sentences.text: Yeah.
SS man.
Yeah.
His name is [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know if he's still alive or not.
Maybe he's alive.
That man did for me a lot.
And he did for other Jewish people, too.
Because in the war time, when you got somebody who helped, I was thinking always maybe I can help somebody else, too.
Then with him, before he gave me food.
And I said later on, you know, I don't need food.
What I will need, a better job what I have.
And maybe you can do something for me.
Only, you when you can, forget about it.
This man, when I told him this, he right away went to the-- my Judenalteste, the leader from the Jewish people from camp there, from Markstadt.
And he told him, you see I am an SS man, he said to him.
He told me this later.
David is my friend.
And I want him to have a good job, the best what you have.
He said, good.
He was afraid for that man.
Good.
And he said, don't mention-- don't call David in office, because-- his name was Judenalteste Baruk Meister.
He was not such a good man.
He didn't treat the Jewish people so good.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then he gave me a job in a little place, like a room.
And over there, came locomotives.
And I have to supply the locomotives with coal and with water-- not by hand, only by buttons.
And he took me in that room-- no, no, a German took me in there.
He gave me the job.
And he was showing me how to work with the pump, with the buttons, with the coal, with the water.
You're pushing here, you will have water.
You're pushing here, the coal will fall in the locomotives there.
And you have everything here.
He was showing me about an hour or two.
And then I got it.
And I started to work.
A German watched me.
I got the job.
Then when I got the job, it was a very good job.
I didn't do-- the whole day, I was looking out or coming-- it came maybe two a day, or three.
Then I gave him water.
He came in the boat.
And he was drinking something, that locomotive man.
He was a Polack or somebody else.
And all coal he needed.
The job was done good.
Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a German leader, he was a meister, they called him, a foreman.
He got, let's say, like, 100 people.
They worked for him.
And he supervised them.
And they told him maybe you can help us from home.
The ghetto existed over there Bedzin and [NON-ENGLISH], Bedzin, Sosnowiec.
We want you to go there and to bring us from their money, watches, a ring.
Anything what they have, they will give it to you.
We will write to them letters.
They gave him letters to that man.
He went to the-- to the ghetto in there.
He took everything for himself.
He didn't want to give nobody nothing.
He brought a bag with, with gold.
But the Jewish people, when they got something to help a brother, a father, they give everything away.
Two guys came to me.
David, you have a job from him.
You know everything.
Help us, too.
What I have-- what I can help you?
They told me the story.
I said, you know what?
I can help you nothing.
Only, I ask him.
He can do it.
Tomorrow, you'll have an answer.
Every morning, I saw him.
He came into me.
In the morning, he comes in.
I said, [INAUDIBLE], sit down.
I have to ask you something.
Put away the rifle.
Sit down.
He said, what do you want, David?
He called me Dah-vid.
I said, listen.
It's happened, this and this.
This and this man from [NON-ENGLISH], he went to Bedzin, Sosnowiec, other cities.
And he took a lot of money and a lot of gold.
And I gave him letters.
And he took everything.
Not I gave him-- I gave it to a man.
The man gave it to him.
And he said, OK.
I'll settle.
I said, how you will settle?
You'll see.
I said, only one condition, I said to him.
Don't mention me.
And still I want to be alive and you, too.
I don't want the Gestapo to know that, or the SS, or other police.
You know, we, we-- the life is every day very dangerous.
I'll make ever thing sure, he said.
He went to that man.
He told him, you have a choice.
I'll shoot you on the spot.
I am SS man, he said.
I [INAUDIBLE] in Stalingrad.
Or you bring back everything what you robbed by the Jewish people, and you will give it to me, he said.
I want us to have it.
And I give you-- this time, he was not living far away-- two hours.
When you will not bring me this back, I'll shoot you.
And the worst thing will happen, I give you to the-- to the Gestapo.
He went home, he bought everything with the letters, everything.
And he gave it to the soldier, to the SS man.
He comes to me, [INAUDIBLE].
He said, David, I have everything already hidden.
What I have to do with it?
I said, listen, I give you two men.
And they will take this.
I will not take this, only two men.
Call two men.
I called in.
I went out.
I said, wait here 10 minutes.
A locomotive will come.
Let them wait.
I'll be back soon.
And I came back with two men.
And I said, you go with that SS man.
And one was afraid.
He was-- I said, don't be afraid.
He is my friend.
He will do nothing to you.
He, he will help you.
And he went with them.
He gave everything.
Take it.
And then maybe six, seven people took this in-- they packed on-- they could not go with a package.
They brought us in the camp.
They got the letters.
The called to people.
They give everything that's belonged to them-- a ring, a watch, money, everything.
Then the Jewish people saw he did so much for them.
Then they told me they want to give them to 200 mark.
I said, if you want to give him, give it to him.
Said, no.
They are afraid to go near him to give him money.
He is still a SS man.
They want to give it to me.
And I have to give it.
I said, that's OK.
In the morning, I'm going out with the 200 mark.
And I always-- see, it was not allowed to have for us money, not even five pfennig-- nothing money.
When they caught you with money, you got punished for that.
They hit you.
They punish you.
Even, they could hang you up there.
You got a [? sturdy ?] gallow there, too.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They gave me the 200 mark.
And I'm going away in the morning with the whole group.
You went 5,000 people to work.
And I came on the [NON-ENGLISH].
I'm going away from that group to my booth where I work.
And I see a other SS man follows me.
And the other one, I knew.
He was the worst SS man from all SS in Markstadt.
Because we knew each other.
He was shooting.
He was killing people.
And I was thinking what to do.
I figured out that he got not to know that I have 200 mark for the other men for the [INAUDIBLE].
And I came in my room.
I went fast.
He was a man fast, too.
And I was faster.
And I came in right near the door.
The floor was from sand.
And I made a little hole.
And I put in the 200 mark there.
And I covered it up.
And he is here right away.
In seconds, he was-- walks in.
Give me the 200 mark.
I said, who told you I have 200 marks?
Nobody gave me.
I don't know.
You dream about something.
I have no money.
He start to hit me.
I took off my clothing.
And he was pushing me.
He was kicking me.
You can shoot me.
I don't know.
I don't have money.
Money?
I don't have even one penny.
What you want from me with, with 200 mark?
Nobody gave it to me.
And I don't know what you're talking.
And he was in almost this time an hour.
And he was bothering me.
I will not walk out from [INAUDIBLE], he said to me.
You would be already dead, he said to me, because with the other friend, he knew it, too.
This, you are still alive.
And I-- in a few minutes, my friend comes in, the other SS man who was in the army with me together.
He said to him, listen, what you want from him?
He works for you?
He said, he works for you.
He said, what we will fighting about here in front of that man?
Come on out.
I want to tell you something.
And he took him out.
He went with him maybe [INAUDIBLE] toward the other way.
And he was talking with him maybe a half hour.
And he came back.
He said, David, don't worry.
I gave him 100 mark.
And I have 100 mark.
This was that.
They liquidated the end of the year the whole camp.
And I suppose to go for to [NON-ENGLISH] where the SS will take over.
And I was worried about [NON-ENGLISH].
A week before, I went on the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I passed a big shot, and I didn't take off my hat.
Every Jew got to take off the hat for, for, for them.
I didn't look.
I didn't take it off.
He took my number.
He, he has my name.
He said, next week, you come again to [INAUDIBLE], you'll be shot on the spot.
You can live out a few days.
I'll let you.
He left.
A SS man, when you look at them, only for looking the people were scared.
I came back.
I got a friend.
He was in the krankenstube.
And he was like a [NON-ENGLISH] we called him.
Somebody got sick, somebody cut his finger, he put a bandage, some doctor.
He was not a doctor.
And I went into the krankenstube to the-- was the-- there was there a man who was a doctor.
Only, he didn't get papers from my town.
See, I was looking to get, get out here alive.
And I told him the story.
His name was Schefter.
And I said-- he didn't got papers.
No, he was a doctor.
We called him at home some name, he got there.
Because he knew everything like a doctor, only he didn't got the papers.
And I told him this story.
And he knew my parents, my brothers.
He knew me from home.
He said, what I can help you?
I said, take me in the krankenstube.
He said, how I can take you in?
You are healthy.
I said, tomorrow I'm going to be sick.
You will take me in.
Then, my-- I got a accident here once.
And it was not healed.
I put a bandage on that.
And I walked in the krankenstube.
He said, lay down on the bed.
Only, I, I can't promise you when the-- tomorrow comes here from Auschwitz a doctor with the-- with the other SS people, and they take you off naked.
And they look at you.
And they will test you.
And they will see that you are sick.
And they will send you away.
Then it's not my fault.
I do my best.
Then everybody in the-- in the camp got to go out and they marched out there to [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was not far away, maybe three, four miles.
They have to walk there.
And they empty everything.
I was sick with other few sick people there in the krankenstube.
From Auschwitz, they're coming in-- a doctor with all the SS people.
They're coming in.
They came in.
Everybody was very, very afraid for them.
Even the Germans were afraid for them.
Need-- I will not say something else now about me.
He said, what's wrong with him?
He said, he was in accident.
No, you don't need him.
He can go to Auschwitz.
Over there is much better than here.
He said, this man, got-- in a day, two, he'll be healthy.
I'm sure.
He said, you a responsible man?
That Schefter, you know, my, my man, my landsman, he said, I am responsible for him.
I know he is healthy.
This, he will heal this in two days, in three days, that's all.
And he said, OK.
Accept this.
You have to go over there.
They got a table in the middle of concentration camp for the sick people.
They took out everyone to the table.
And they talked over, over the three or four people by the table, or he got to go to Auschwitz or he got to go to work.
When I came there, and then I was-- I was going my foot like this, not so good.
And he said, what's the matter with him?
And the Schefter was there.
He said, he's all right.
The same [INAUDIBLE].
He's healthy.
He will go back not two, three days to work.
You are responsible.
I am responsible.
Only when I left, I went a little bit faster.
Then the Wachtmeister was calling me back, the man, the SS man.
When he called me back, he said, eh, I have to go.
Then he changed his mind, had to go.
Then I went into the krankenstube.
Then from there, I went to Blechhammer.
When I came to, to Blechhammer, it was also there 5,000 people.
It was a much better camp then Markstadt.
It was from the SS more organized with food and with cleaness.
It was everything clean.
People took showers after work if they wanted.
And it, it was discipline 100%.
You have to obey everything.
And we got there, our Judenalteste, Jewish leader, his name was Demra.
He went to Israel after the war, Demra.
He was also from Sosnowiec.
I heared about him home before the war.
And, and, and this man, first of all, he said to all the kapos-- you know what a kapo is?
A kapo is a police people what they were watching.
He told them, I don't want you to hit Jewish people.
You can talk to them.
You can do everything.
Something is serious, come to me and I'll settle.
And he was a good leader, a good leader.
And this was a good camp.
And then over there, I have to come to [? end. ?] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We got not better.
It was a concentration camp with discipline with everything.
They hang people every, every at least once a week, two, three people.
This, I have to tell you.
I was in a room, maybe 32 or 35 people.
It was with me a man.
He was a lawyer from Vienna.
And his name was Oxenhall.
Oxenhall from Vienna.
And this man was a kapo because a kapo didn't do nothing.
Only, he was a good one, like a diamond, very good one.
When it was winter time, we went to work around four, five miles.
When we came-- people, they gave in the morning soup, everybody. 99% ate the soup. 1% Took the soup with them for lunch because we didn't get lunch there.
Then they didn't eat nothing in the morning.
They eat the soup.
They warmed up, and they eat there.
Then it was there a Jew-- I forgot his name.
He was from Bedzin, Poland.
And then was a other Jew.
He was from Paris, [INAUDIBLE].
And he was-- I mention this people, because something happened to them.
And Oxenhall, who was a kapo and a commandant-- I think 34-- and he, he was good and very gentle and nice and everything.
And once, one went with the-- they have to carry the, the-- we called this a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know how they call this in, in English, the pot they put in the soup and they [INAUDIBLE] by hand.
We didn't got gloves.
And winter was cold.
They were freezing the hand.
They didn't know what to do.
When one passed from Bedzin, the men, he's [INAUDIBLE] on the floor.
Then he went, he took a piece-- ripped off.
He took a rock and knocked it-- a wire.
And he put it here on the wire and got already that saved his hand from the cold.
And the man from Paris, the French man, the Jew, did the same thing.
And they were walking like that with the soup.
Then was running after a civilian, a German.
He said, what you did here with the wires?
This is a wire connected from one bunker to the other bunker.
And you made sabotage.
Then he said, it's cold.
It's winter.
They didn't know it's a wire like this.
The lawyer came.
He talked, they're innocent.
One is mental sick.
And the other one didn't know what he is doing.
Then he took his name, and his name, and his number, and his number, and, and the lawyer's number.
We came to camp.
They took away all three.
We didn't see them for two weeks.
After two weeks, we coming home from work, 5,000 people-- winter, cold.
We're staying outside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're waiting.
They don't let us in the barracks.
Then came a station wagon.
It comes out men that chained in the back, the hands, all three of them. the lawyer, the French Jew, the Polish Jew.
And they take them to the gallows.
And the gallows was already prepared for the three people to hang them.
And we, all 5,000 people, will have to stay.
And I, my place, was just in the front, like here.
And here they were hanging them.
They put me there.
People c-- 5,000 people you putting in place, that took 10, 15 minutes.
And, and I have to stay and to see like that.
And it was there a helper.
I don't know or he volunteered or not.
And his name was Polack.
And I think he was-- no, no, no.
He's no Polack.
His name was Schwartzbaum.
And he was from Czestochowa.
And he helped hang the people.
And everybody was so excited that a Jew helps them to help.
I don't know if they forced him to do it or he volunteered this to do it.
If he, he volunteered, he got a bread for every Jew got killed.
Then he got three breads.
Until today, I don't know or he volunteer or they forced him to do it.
And they hanged all three people.
They knocked off the chairs there.
And they were hanging.
And the man, the Oxenhall, before they knocked out the chairs, he was begging them to-- you got a collar here-- to put the around the neck, that knot.
Not on the collar, under collar.
Because he was thinking about a fast death.
Not to-- not to--
Yeah.
No.
Blechhammer, Blechhammer, I was a year.
I came from Markstadt, I was longer.
Death march.
The death march, we going out-- it was-- they gave out food, empty the, the, the SS from magazine.
People take out there-- 20 minutes, 15 .
And the food, they gave everybody a bread.
And it was happened, somebody steal the bread.
He was running.
And a Nazi sh-- killed him, shot him.
But I saw it.
And we went out 5,000 people from that camp on the death march.
When we went out, the leader, he was a big-- how you call this-- dog.
And we went around the camp like this once.
And then he didn't know.
Comes people and they tell him something.
Later one, we knew it what was wrong.
Because the Russians were very near.
And we want to go a different way.
They want to go a different way.
And he changed strategy to go-- to go a other way.
And we started to go, the 5,000 people.
From there, we went-- I'll make this the short.
It was winter.
It was cold already.
We stopped in in camps like Gross Rosen.
It was a big camp there.
